description: It's your first day as an exchange student at a French university. Navigate through the day by choosing the right responses!

steps.situation: You step out of your apartment and see your neighbor in the hallway.

choices.text: Walk past quickly without making eye contact
choices.feedback: Ignoring neighbors is considered rude in France. Even a simple greeting makes a big difference!

choices.text: Say 'Bonjour!' with a smile and nod
choices.feedback: Parfait! A friendly 'Bonjour' is essential. Your neighbor will appreciate the politeness. This small gesture builds good relationships.

choices.text: Give them a big hug
choices.feedback: Hugging strangers or neighbors you just met is too familiar in French culture. A simple 'Bonjour' and smile is much more appropriate.

steps.situation: You stop at the boulangerie for breakfast. There's a queue of 3 people ahead of you.

choices.text: Walk up to the counter and quickly order
choices.feedback: Cutting in line is extremely rude in France! Always wait your turn in the queue.

choices.text: Wait in line, then say 'Bonjour! Un pain au chocolat, s'il vous plaît'
choices.feedback: Très bien! You greeted the shopkeeper, waited your turn, and ordered politely. You're fitting in perfectly!

choices.text: Wait in line, then point at what you want without speaking
choices.feedback: While waiting in line is correct, you should always greet with 'Bonjour' first and use words to order. Pointing without speaking is considered impolite.

bestPhrase.french: Bonjour! Un pain au chocolat et un café, s'il vous plaît.
bestPhrase.english: Hello! A chocolate croissant and a coffee, please.

steps.situation: You arrive at your first class. The professor is already at the front. You're 2 minutes late.

choices.text: Walk in loudly, find a seat, and sit down without acknowledging the professor
choices.feedback: Entering loudly without acknowledgment disrupts the class and is disrespectful to the professor.

choices.text: Enter quietly, nod to the professor, and take the nearest seat
choices.feedback: Bien fait! Entering discreetly and acknowledging the professor shows respect. Being a few minutes late is generally acceptable, but try to be on time.

choices.text: Wait outside until there's a break to enter
choices.feedback: While thoughtful, waiting too long means you miss content. It's better to enter quietly and sit down discreetly. A small nod to the professor is sufficient.

steps.situation: A French student sitting next to you turns and introduces themselves: 'Salut, moi c'est Marie!'

choices.text: Reply in English: 'Hi, I'm [name], nice to meet you!'
choices.feedback: While not terrible, switching to English immediately might create distance. Try French first - it shows effort and respect!

choices.text: Reply: 'Salut Marie! Moi c'est [name]. Je suis étudiant(e) en échange. Tu es en quelle filière?'
choices.feedback: Excellent! You replied in French, introduced yourself, and asked about their program. This opens the door to a great friendship!

choices.text: Just smile and nod nervously
choices.feedback: It's normal to be nervous, but try to respond! Even a simple 'Salut! Moi c'est [name]' is enough. French students appreciate any effort.

bestPhrase.french: Salut! Moi c'est [nom]. Enchanté(e)! Tu es en quelle filière?
bestPhrase.english: Hi! I'm [name]. Nice to meet you! What program are you in?

steps.situation: You're at the university cafeteria. A group of French students invites you to sit with them for lunch.

choices.text: Say 'No thanks' and eat alone looking at your phone
choices.feedback: Declining a lunch invitation can seem distant. French meals are deeply social - it's a great chance to make friends and practice French!

choices.text: Say 'Oui, avec plaisir! Merci!' and join them
choices.feedback: Parfait! French meals are social events. By joining, you'll make friends, practice French, and understand student culture. Enjoy the conversation!

choices.text: Join but keep your earphones in and eat quickly
choices.feedback: Eating with earphones in and rushing through the meal goes against French dining culture. Meals are for conversation and socializing.

bestPhrase.french: Oui, avec plaisir! Merci de m'inviter. Bon appétit!
bestPhrase.english: Yes, with pleasure! Thanks for inviting me. Enjoy your meal!

steps.situation: You need to submit enrollment documents. The administrative assistant looks busy.

choices.text: Walk up and immediately explain what you need in English
choices.feedback: Starting in English without a greeting can seem demanding. Always begin with 'Bonjour' and try French first.

choices.text: Say 'Bonjour Madame, excusez-moi de vous déranger. Je voudrais déposer mes documents d'inscription.'
choices.feedback: Excellent! You greeted politely, apologized for the interruption, and stated your need clearly. This is perfect French etiquette in administrative settings.

choices.text: Wait silently until they notice you
choices.feedback: While patient, you might wait forever! It's perfectly fine to politely get their attention with 'Bonjour' and 'Excusez-moi.'

bestPhrase.french: Bonjour Madame, excusez-moi de vous déranger. Je voudrais déposer mes documents d'inscription, s'il vous plaît.
bestPhrase.english: Hello Madam, sorry to bother you. I'd like to submit my enrollment documents, please.

steps.situation: You're buying groceries. At checkout, the cashier greets you with 'Bonjour!'

choices.text: Stay silent and put your items on the belt
choices.feedback: Not responding to 'Bonjour' is considered one of the rudest things you can do in France. Always return the greeting!

choices.text: Reply 'Bonjour!' and put your items on the belt. After paying, say 'Merci, bonne soirée!'
choices.feedback: Parfait! Greeting back and saying goodbye with 'bonne soirée' (good evening) shows perfect French manners. Small interactions like these matter!

choices.text: Nod and smile but don't say anything
choices.feedback: A nod is better than nothing, but verbal greetings are expected in France. A simple 'Bonjour' takes one second and makes a huge difference.

bestPhrase.english: Hello! ... Thank you very much, have a good evening!

steps.situation: Your new French friend Marie texts you: 'Salut! On fait une soirée chez Thomas ce soir à 20h. Tu viens?' (Hey! We're having a party at Thomas's tonight at 8 PM. Coming?)

choices.text: Reply: 'Oui, je viens! J'apporte quelque chose?' and arrive at exactly 8:00 PM
choices.feedback: Good that you offered to bring something! But arriving exactly at 8:00 might be too early. For French social gatherings, 10-15 minutes late is the norm.

choices.text: Reply: 'Oui, avec plaisir! Je peux apporter du vin?' and arrive around 8:15 PM
choices.feedback: Parfait! You accepted enthusiastically, offered to bring wine (always appreciated!), and arrived at the socially appropriate 'quart d'heure de politesse.' You're becoming truly French!

choices.text: Don't reply and just show up whenever
choices.feedback: Not confirming and showing up randomly is inconsiderate. Always respond to invitations, even if just to say you'll try to come.

bestPhrase.french: Oui, avec plaisir! Je peux apporter une bouteille de vin. À ce soir!
bestPhrase.english: Yes, with pleasure! I can bring a bottle of wine. See you tonight!

excellent.title: True Parisien(ne)!
excellent.message: You navigated your first day like a local! Your French manners are impeccable. You'll fit right in.

good.title: Bon Début!
good.message: Good start! You made some great choices but there's room to improve. Focus on always greeting people and trying French first.

needsWork.title: Keep Practicing!
needsWork.message: You have some French culture basics but need more practice. Review the Culture Guide section to learn about French etiquette.

beginner.title: Bienvenue en France!
beginner.message: Looks like French culture is still new to you — that's totally okay! Start with our Culture Guide and French Phrases sections to get prepared.
